description: End-to-end evaluation cases for the Reaper research pipeline (Horizon 1)

test_cases.name: Cryptographic construction with a known proof gap
test_cases.goal: Verify whether the security proof in the main theorem holds under the stated threat model. Identify any gaps, missing cases, or implicit assumptions.

expected_outputs.paper-summary: notes/paper-summary.md must contain verbatim theorem statements, complete system model, and red flags section identifying the proof gap
expected_outputs.literature: notes/literature.md must contain at least 10 related works with specific relevance statements
expected_outputs.hypotheses: notes/problem-statement.md must contain falsifiable claims with explicit success/failure conditions targeting the proof gap
expected_outputs.results: notes/results.md must have at least 5 cycle rows with keep/discard decisions
expected_outputs.current-understanding: notes/current-understanding.md must identify the specific proof gap (not just 'there may be issues')
expected_outputs.report: report.md must have a one-sentence central finding, refutable contributions, and concrete examples

quality_criteria: The proof gap is identified with specificity (which lemma, which step, what goes wrong)
The report explains the gap with a concrete execution trace or adversary strategy
current-understanding.md only advances on keep cycles (verify via notes/results.md cross-check)

suggested_papers.title: Papers with known simulator construction issues (e.g., missing abort handling)
suggested_papers.where_to_find: IACR ePrint, search for papers with published errata or follow-up corrections

test_cases.name: Consensus protocol comparison (e.g., HotStuff variants)
test_cases.goal: Compare the security properties and performance of this protocol against its closest competitors. Identify any claims that don't hold under fair comparison (same threat model).

expected_outputs.paper-summary: notes/paper-summary.md must include communication/round complexity claims and the exact network/adversary model
expected_outputs.literature: notes/literature.md must cover competing protocols (PBFT, Tendermint, HotStuff, etc.) with consistent comparison dimensions
expected_outputs.hypotheses: notes/problem-statement.md must include claims about relative performance under the same model
expected_outputs.results: notes/results.md must show comparison cycles using consistent dimensions from methodology.md
expected_outputs.current-understanding: notes/current-understanding.md must contain a structured comparison table
expected_outputs.report: report.md must identify at least one unfair or misleading comparison in the original paper (if any)

quality_criteria: Comparisons use the same threat model for all protocols (flagged if original paper doesn't)
Complexity claims are verified, not just repeated from the paper
The report distinguishes between theoretical bounds and practical performance
Literature review covers the protocol's lineage (what it builds on, what builds on it)

suggested_papers.title: HotStuff (Yin et al., PODC 2019) or variants like Jolteon, Ditto, BullShark

test_cases.name: Blockchain paper with questionable security claims
test_cases.goal: Evaluate the security claims of this blockchain protocol. Determine whether the claimed properties actually hold under the stated assumptions, and whether the assumptions are realistic.

expected_outputs.paper-summary: notes/paper-summary.md must flag any red flags (synchrony/asynchrony mismatch, missing liveness argument, vague security definition)
expected_outputs.literature: notes/literature.md must include relevant impossibility results and known attacks on similar constructions
expected_outputs.hypotheses: notes/problem-statement.md must include hypotheses about whether specific claims are valid
expected_outputs.results: notes/results.md must show at least one counterexample search cycle
expected_outputs.current-understanding: notes/current-understanding.md must clearly state which claims hold, which fail, and which are undetermined
expected_outputs.report: report.md must present any counterexample with a concrete execution trace

quality_criteria: At least one counterexample search is attempted (not just proof verification)
Impossibility results from literature are applied to narrow the search space
The report is honest — if the paper is actually secure, say so; don't manufacture flaws
Assumptions are evaluated for realism (e.g., synchrony in a WAN deployment)

suggested_papers.title: Blockchain papers from non-top-tier venues with strong security claims but limited formalization
suggested_papers.where_to_find: arXiv cs.CR, IEEE Blockchain conference proceedings

skill_unit_tests.test: Given any crypto/distributed systems paper, paper-summary.md must have all sections filled (Metadata, Problem Statement, System Model with all 5 sub-dimensions, Construction Overview, Claimed Security Properties with verbatim theorems, Proof Technique, Complexity Claims, Limitations, Key Definitions, Red Flags)

Given a research goal and paper-summary.md, literature.md must have a landscape summary, at least 10 related works table entries with non-empty Relevance column, key prior results, and at least 2 specific gaps

Given paper-summary.md and literature.md, problem-statement.md must have complete trust assumptions (all 5 dimensions), at least 2 ideas each with statement/success/failure conditions, and priorities justified by consequence

After N cycles: notes/results.md has one row per hypothesis (updated inline on revisit, not duplicated), investigation directories 001 through NNN exist with analysis.md files, current-understanding.md changed only on keep cycles, every cycle outcome is statable in one sentence
report.md has a one-sentence central finding, bulleted refutable contributions, each finding starts with a concrete example, no chronological narration, open questions are specific

search command returns valid JSON array with fields: arxiv_id, title, authors, year, abstract, pdf_url. citations command returns references and citations arrays. Tested via: python -m pytest tests/test_search_arxiv.py
search command returns valid JSON array with fields: eprint_id, title, pdf_url, url. url command returns correct ePrint URLs. Tested via: python -m pytest tests/test_search_iacr.py

literature.md includes papers found via arXiv and ePrint search scripts (not just WebSearch). Citation Graph section present with forward/backward citations. Graceful degradation: pipeline completes with WebSearch fallback when scripts unavailable.

When stuck at step 4, mid-cycle literature search uses search scripts. New papers integrated into literature.md inline (added to appropriate existing sections). literature-search action-type logged in notes/results.md.

evaluation_protocol.setup:
1. Place a test paper PDF in dev/test-papers/ with the filename matching the test case
2. Open Claude Code in the repo root
evaluation_protocol.evaluate: After the pipeline completes, check each expected_output and quality_criteria. Score pass/fail per criterion.
evaluation_protocol.iterate: For each failure, identify whether the issue is in the skill prompt, the orchestrator composition, or the model's capability. Fix skill prompts first.
